figures.caption: Fig. 1: Inference timelines under two regimes. (a) EXEintensive pipelining where transfers are fully hidden. (b) DMA-intensive pipelining where transfer overhead is unavoidable.
Fig. 8: Module residency comparison. Keeping ViT and Expert offloaded (CC) yields the lowest latency for any given VRAM budget, confirming the theoretical residency benefit analysis.
Fig. 6: Residency benefit per module versus output token count (Sdiff=10). VLM benefit exceeds Diffusion at N>10 and dominates at Alpamayo’s typical N=21.

Fig. 7: Distribution of VLM output token counts across 100 randomly sampled driving clips. All inputs produce N ≥ 11 tokens (median=15, mean=15.7), well above the N=10 threshold where VLM residency benefit exceeds Diffusion.
Fig. 2: VRAM–latency tradeoff of Hugging Face Accelerate offloading under varying VRAM limits on RTX 5070 Ti.
Fig. 9: Progressive optimization: Baseline → Sequential DL → Pipelined DL → Ours (Nr=28). Each stage improves latency; resident layer selection provides the largest gain (2.67×).
Fig. 10: VRAM–latency tradeoff. Our method consistently achieves 3.5–3.6× lower latency than Accelerate offloading at every VRAM budget.
Fig. 3: Average per-layer DMA and execution times for each Alpamayo module (RTX 5070 Ti). ViT and VLM Prefill are EXE-intensive (CDMA < CEXE); VLM Decode and Expert are DMA-intensive (CDMA > CEXE).
Fig. 11: Comparison of baseline and DFB on RTX 3080 Ti.
